heroOverview: Rhinophyma is a late-stage manifestation of rosacea characterised by progressive, irregular thickening and enlargement of the nasal skin, driven by sebaceous gland hyperplasia and connective tissue overgrowth. It develops almost exclusively in men — the male-to-female ratio is approximately 12:1 — typically after years of inadequately treated subtype 1 or 2 rosacea. The nose becomes bulbous, pitted, and deeply furrowed, with a reddened, waxy surface; the condition is progressive without intervention and rarely if ever resolves spontaneously. Surgical resurfacing or ablation is the definitive treatment, producing a markedly improved cosmetic outcome, while controlling underlying rosacea slows or prevents progression.

keyStats.prevalence: Rhinophyma affects an estimated 1–3 per 1,000 people in Western populations; exact figures are limited by under-reporting (National Rosacea Society, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects men, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 12:1; onset typically in the 50s–70s after years of rosacea
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases diagnosed between ages 50 and 70
keyStats.globalCases: Occurs in a subset of the approximately 415 million rosacea patients worldwide; exact rhinophyma prevalence data are sparse

definition: Rhinophyma (ICD-10: L71.1) is a fibrous hyperplasia of the sebaceous glands and connective tissue of the nasal skin, producing asymmetric lobular enlargement, surface irregularity, and progressive disfigurement of the nose. It is classified within the phymatous subtype of rosacea under the 2017 Global ROSacea COnsensus (ROSCO) classification and as subtype 3 in older terminology. Histologically, the lesion shows massively dilated sebaceous glands, follicular plugging with keratin and sebum, prominent vascular dilation (telangiectasias), and diffuse fibrosis of the dermis; the overlying epidermis is preserved but distorted by the expanding dermis beneath. Sebaceous gland units can enlarge to many times their normal diameter. Although historically — and incorrectly — attributed to alcohol excess, alcohol does not cause rhinophyma; the myth arises from the superficially similar facial redness of both rosacea and alcohol-induced vasodilation. The condition falls within dermatology for medical management and is treated surgically by plastic surgeons, otorhinolaryngologists (ENT surgeons), or dermatological surgeons skilled in CO2 laser or electrosurgical ablation for definitive correction. It is distinct from hypertrophic rosacea of other facial regions (phymas can also develop on the chin, ear, and forehead, but nasal involvement is by far the most common). Left untreated, rhinophyma progresses over years, eventually distorting the nasal valve and causing functional obstruction to breathing.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, characterised by prominent enlarged sebaceous glands producing a nodular, pitted surface with visible dilated follicular openings. The skin has a waxy, yellowish sheen and bleeds easily when incised.
Dense dermal fibrosis predominates over glandular change, producing a firmer, harder consistency with less exudate on incision. Associated with more significant structural nasal distortion.
Combined fibrosis and prominent telangiectatic vasculature with a deeply reddened hue; the angiofibrotic stroma is more vascular than pure fibrous rhinophyma and bleeds more during surgery.

A rarer variant associated with significant cumulative UV damage in addition to rosacea; the skin may show solar elastosis, lichenification, and keratoses overlying the typical rhinophymatous changes.

primarySymptoms: Progressive nasal enlargement with irregular, lobular surface contour, typically affecting the tip and alae — the most striking visual feature that patients and observers notice first.
Deep skin pores and follicular openings on the nasal surface, often capped with sebaceous plugs or comedone-like material.
A waxy, shiny or greasy nasal skin surface reflecting the hyperactive underlying sebaceous glands.
Reddening (erythema) of the nasal skin overlying the rhinophymatous changes, continuous with the background rosacea.
Visible telangiectasias (dilated small blood vessels) on the nasal surface and surrounding cheeks.
Functional nasal obstruction in advanced cases as the enlarging soft tissue distorts the external nasal valve and narrows the nasal airway.
Sebaceous material that can be expressed from enlarged pores, sometimes with crusting or secondary surface infection.
Social and psychological distress — including social withdrawal, reduced quality of life, and stigma — rated by affected patients as one of the most burdensome aspects of the condition.
Intermittent oozing or bleeding from surface capillaries, particularly after trauma or temperature extremes.
Skin surface irregularity and firm texture that distinguish it from simple nasal oedema or inflammatory rosacea.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent nasal skin thickening and a roughened or pitted texture in a person with established rosacea.
Gradual widening of nasal pores or new visible sebaceous gland openings on the nasal tip.
Slowly progressive nasal redness that fails to clear between rosacea flares.
A mild but definite asymmetric enlargement of the nasal tip noticed in annual photographs compared with previous years.
emergencySigns: Rapid asymmetric growth of a nasal nodule, which may indicate basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma arising within rhinophymatous tissue rather than typical rhinophyma progression.
Ulceration, bleeding, or crusting of a discrete lesion on the nasal surface — skin cancer must be excluded by biopsy.
Severe nasal airway obstruction causing respiratory difficulty, particularly during sleep.
Signs of secondary bacterial cellulitis — spreading warmth, swelling, and tenderness — requiring prompt antibiotic treatment.

causes.description: Rhinophyma is the fibrohyperplastic end-stage of rosacea; years of chronic follicular inflammation, sebaceous gland activation, and dermal vasodilation trigger progressive sebaceous hyperplasia and fibrous deposition. The underlying Demodex folliculorum colonisation, innate immune activation (Toll-like receptor 2 pathway), and cutaneous dysbiosis that drive rosacea also fuel rhinophyma development.

Sebaceous glands are androgen-sensitive; male sex hormones explain the striking male predominance. Dihydrotestosterone stimulates sebocyte proliferation and sebum production, which are already elevated in rosacea, and the additive effect promotes gland hyperplasia. This mechanism also underlies the rare female cases, many of whom have hormonal abnormalities.

Cumulative ultraviolet radiation damages dermal collagen and stimulates aberrant connective tissue remodelling. UV exposure is an established rosacea trigger, and those with occupational or recreational UV exposure (outdoor workers, sailors) appear at higher risk of the more severe phymatous changes.

Family clustering of rosacea and rhinophyma suggests heritable components. Variants in genes governing innate immune signalling, vascular tone (VEGF pathway), and filaggrin (skin barrier function) have been associated with rosacea susceptibility. The specific genetic architecture of rhinophyma is not fully characterised.

Demodex folliculorum and Demodex brevis are present at markedly elevated densities in rosacea skin compared with controls. The mites breach the follicular epithelium, trigger keratinocyte damage and neutrophil recruitment, and release bacterial antigens that activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, promoting fibrosis and sebaceous hyperplasia.

riskFactors.description: Male sex confers a 12-fold higher risk than female sex, reflecting androgen-driven sebaceous stimulation; nearly all reported rhinophyma cases are in men, and the few female cases are often associated with androgen excess.

Rhinophyma does not arise de novo — virtually all cases occur in men with years of pre-existing erythematotelangiectatic or papulopustular rosacea. The severity and duration of rosacea correlate with the likelihood of phymatous change.

riskFactors.factor: Fair skin and Northern European ancestry
riskFactors.description: Rosacea and its complications are most prevalent in people with Fitzpatrick skin types I–II (Irish, Scottish, Scandinavian heritage), reflecting greater UV sensitivity and possibly genetic rosacea susceptibility.
Rhinophyma typically takes decades of rosacea to develop; peak incidence is in the sixth and seventh decades, making age one of the strongest predictors after male sex and existing rosacea.

Consistent medical management of rosacea — including topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, or ivermectin, and avoidance of triggers — can slow progression to the phymatous stage. Patients who do not seek or maintain treatment are more likely to develop rhinophyma.

Outdoor occupations and recreational sun exposure are rosacea triggers and promote actinic dermal damage that facilitates connective tissue remodelling and sebaceous hyperplasia. Daily SPF 30+ broad-spectrum sunscreen significantly reduces this risk.

While alcohol does not cause rhinophyma, it is a potent rosacea trigger that increases frequency and severity of flushing and vascular dilation, thereby accelerating the inflammatory cycles that drive sebaceous and fibrotic changes. Reducing intake lowers rosacea flare frequency.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 50–70 with longstanding rosacea, the primary affected group by a wide margin
Men of Celtic, Scandinavian, or other Northern European ancestry with fair skin
Outdoor workers and individuals with high lifetime UV exposure
Women with rosacea and androgen excess (rare minority of cases)
Patients whose rosacea has been undertreated or who had late diagnosis
diagnosisOverview: Rhinophyma is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic appearance of progressive nasal skin thickening with sebaceous hyperplasia, lobular contour change, and reddening in the context of longstanding rosacea. No laboratory or imaging workup is required for typical presentations. The clinician should assess the extent of nasal distortion, the degree of functional nasal obstruction (asking about sleep quality and nasal breathing), and the extent of background rosacea on the rest of the face. Dermoscopy shows polygonal scales, follicular plugging, and prominent telangiectasias. Biopsy is performed when the differential diagnosis includes malignancy — particularly basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma, which can arise within rhinophymatous tissue and present as an ulcerated or rapidly growing nodule. Biopsy is also needed when the histological subtype would guide surgical approach (glandular versus fibrous). Differential diagnosis requires excluding other causes of nasal enlargement: sarcoidosis (lupus pernio), granulomatous infections (leishmaniasis, leprosy), lymphoma infiltration, and primary skin tumours. Photography under standardised conditions at each visit documents progression and aids surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses rhinophyma by identifying the characteristic sebaceous hyperplasia, follicular plugging, telangiectasias, and lobular contour change under 10–20× magnification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist examines the nose with a handheld dermoscope; no preparation is needed. Takes 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma when there is ulceration, bleeding, a discrete growing nodule, or clinical suspicion of malignancy within the rhinophymatous tissue.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A local anaesthetic injection is given; a small core of tissue (2–4 mm) is removed with a punch biopsy tool or shave technique. The site heals in 1–2 weeks; histology results take 5–7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the degree of internal nasal airway compromise from external soft tissue distortion of the nasal valve, important when the patient reports mouth breathing or sleep-disordered breathing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible or rigid endoscope is passed through the nostril after a topical decongestant; takes 5–10 minutes in the outpatient clinic.

diagnosticTests.test: Photography for staging and surgical planning
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised frontal, profile, and caudal photographs document severity, track progression, and guide the surgical team in planning the extent of resection needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinical photographs taken in the clinic under standardised lighting; no discomfort. Usually repeated at each follow-up visit.

diagnosticTests.test: Rosacea severity assessment (IGA scale or DLQI)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies background rosacea severity and the impact on quality of life to guide medical treatment of the underlying condition alongside surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief questionnaire completed by the patient and a structured clinical grading of erythema, papules, pustules, and phymatous change; takes about 5 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of rhinophyma is two-pronged: medical management of the underlying rosacea to halt progression, and surgical or energy-based ablation of the hyperplastic nasal tissue to restore contour. Medical therapy with topical metronidazole (0.75–1%), azelaic acid (15–20%), or topical ivermectin (1%) reduces rosacea inflammation and may slow early sebaceous changes, but no topical or oral therapy reverses established rhinophyma. Oral isotretinoin (0.1–0.5 mg/kg/day) can reduce sebaceous gland activity and, in early or mild cases, meaningfully shrink rhinophymatous tissue; it is most useful as an adjunct before or after surgery to reduce recurrence risk. The mainstay of treatment for established rhinophyma is surgical reshaping. Carbon dioxide (CO2) laser ablation and electrosurgical loop excision are the two most widely used techniques — both remove excess soft tissue layer by layer under local or general anaesthesia, preserving normal nasal architecture while eliminating the hyperplastic sebaceous component. The CO2 laser offers precise tissue removal and simultaneous haemostasis; most patients require one session and heal over 2–4 weeks. Full-thickness excision with skin grafting is reserved for the most severe cases with deep fibrosis. Cryotherapy, radiofrequency dermabrasion, and scalpel excision are additional options. Without surgical treatment, rhinophyma progresses slowly but continuously; with good surgical management, long-term cosmetic and functional outcomes are excellent.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical ivermectin 1% cream
medicalTreatments.description: A topical anti-parasitic and anti-inflammatory agent that reduces Demodex mite density and dampens innate immune activation driving rosacea inflammation. Applied once daily; primary treatment for the inflammatory background rosacea component rather than established tissue hyperplasia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces rosacea inflammatory lesion count by approximately 76% at 16 weeks versus vehicle in phase III trials (Galderma studies, 2015); effect on established rhinophyma is modest.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical metronidazole (0.75–1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces rosacea papules and pustules and, with long-term use, may slow the inflammatory drive to phymatous change. Applied once or twice daily; well tolerated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces rosacea lesion count by 40–65% versus placebo; maintenance use reduces relapse rate by approximately 50% in extension studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral isotretinoin (low-dose)
medicalTreatments.description: A vitamin A derivative that suppresses sebaceous gland size and sebum output by 75–90%; at low doses (0.1–0.3 mg/kg/day) can reduce sebaceous hyperplasia in early rhinophyma and is useful pre- or post-surgically to extend remission. Teratogenic; requires pregnancy prevention in women.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nasal sebaceous volume and surface irregularity in approximately 50–70% of patients with early or moderate rhinophyma after 4–6 months of treatment at low doses.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral doxycycline (40 mg modified-release)
medicalTreatments.description: Sub-antimicrobial doxycycline suppresses matrix metalloproteinases and reduces rosacea inflammation and erythema without driving antibiotic resistance. Used for background rosacea control rather than rhinophyma reversal.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces rosacea papule and pustule counts by approximately 40–60% at 16 weeks; approved specifically for rosacea at 40 mg modified-release (Oracea).

surgicalOptions.name: CO2 laser ablation
surgicalOptions.description: A focused carbon dioxide laser precisely vaporises excess sebaceous tissue layer by layer, sculpting the nose to a normal contour while coagulating bleeding vessels. Performed under local or general anaesthesia in a single session; re-epithelialisation occurs over 2–4 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cosmetic satisfaction rates of 85–95% in published case series; recurrence is uncommon (5–15% at 5 years) and addressable with further ablation or low-dose oral isotretinoin.

surgicalOptions.name: Electrosurgical excision and loop dermabrasion
surgicalOptions.description: A cutting loop or blade removes excess tissue followed by electrosurgical feathering of the surface to achieve a smooth contour. Lower equipment cost than laser; technically versatile. Healing takes 2–4 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good-to-excellent cosmetic outcomes in approximately 80–90% of patients; comparable to laser in skilled hands.

surgicalOptions.name: Scalpel excision with primary closure or grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Full-thickness excision used for severe, deeply fibrotic rhinophyma; skin defect is repaired with a local advancement flap or split-thickness skin graft. More invasive and carries a higher risk of visible scarring compared to ablative techniques.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional reconstruction is successful in nearly all cases; aesthetic outcomes are generally inferior to ablative techniques but necessary for the most advanced disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Radiofrequency ablation
alternativeTreatments.description: Radiofrequency energy delivered through a fine electrode selectively ablates excess tissue; similar in concept to electrosurgery but allows more precise titration of energy. Used for mild to moderate rhinophyma with comparable healing times to CO2 laser.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cryotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Liquid nitrogen freezing reduces soft tissue volume over multiple sessions through controlled tissue destruction. Less precise than laser; useful for very mild cases or patients unsuitable for surgery, but cosmetic outcomes are generally inferior.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intense pulsed light (IPL) and vascular lasers
alternativeTreatments.description: Targeting the vascular component (telangiectasias, erythema) of the rosacea-rhinophyma spectrum; IPL and pulsed dye laser reduce redness and slow inflammatory activity but do not reduce established sebaceous hyperplasia.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with established rhinophyma should see a dermatologist to confirm the diagnosis, exclude malignancy, and manage the underlying rosacea medically. Surgical referral — to a plastic surgeon, ENT, or dermatological surgeon with laser experience — is indicated whenever the nasal deformity is causing cosmetic distress or functional obstruction. Early surgical intervention, when tissue changes are less fibrotic, generally produces better outcomes with shorter healing than waiting until disease is severe.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a dermatologist experienced in rosacea management for medical control of the underlying condition, combined with a surgical specialist who performs nasal resurfacing regularly — ideally a dermatological surgeon, plastic surgeon, or ENT surgeon with dedicated laser or electrosurgical rhinophyma experience. Enquire specifically about the volume of rhinophyma cases they treat annually and whether they have a CO2 laser. For suspected malignancy, a Mohs surgeon or dermatopathology-linked dermatologist is preferred.
preventionStrategies: Diagnose and consistently treat rosacea early — topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, or ivermectin used long-term can slow or prevent progression to phymatous disease.
Identify and avoid personal rosacea triggers: sun exposure, heat, spicy foods, alcohol, and extreme temperatures are the most common.
Use daily broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen on the face year-round; UV damage is a key accelerant of rosacea-related sebaceous hyperplasia.
Attend annual dermatology review if you have rosacea and are male — early detection of phymatous changes allows intervention before significant enlargement.
Limit alcohol consumption, since it triggers flushing and vascular dilation that fuel rosacea progression.
Use gentle, non-comedogenic skincare and avoid vigorous scrubbing of the nose, which can worsen follicular disruption.
lifestyleModifications: Keep a rosacea trigger diary: typical triggers include hot beverages, spicy food, alcohol, hot baths, cold wind, and emotional stress. Identifying and avoiding personal triggers reduces flare frequency.
Apply SPF 30+ mineral sunscreen (zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) every morning as a standard step; UV is the single most consistent rosacea trigger.
Wash the face with a pH-balanced, fragrance-free cleanser; avoid foaming cleansers with sodium lauryl sulphate, which disrupt the skin barrier.
Use non-comedogenic, fragrance-free moisturisers that do not occlude follicular pores.
Limit alcohol, particularly red wine, which triggers vasodilation more than other types.
Follow up with a dermatologist at least once a year to reassess phymatous change and adjust topical or oral rosacea treatment.

recommended: Cooling, mild foods that do not trigger facial flushing — cold water, lukewarm foods, bland seasonings
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish, flaxseed, and walnuts, which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rosacea
Plenty of fruits and vegetables providing antioxidants that limit UV-mediated skin damage
Green tea (lukewarm, not hot) — contains polyphenols with anti-inflammatory and anti-angiogenic properties relevant to rosacea
avoid: Hot beverages and soups, which are among the most consistent rosacea-flush triggers
Spicy foods (capsaicin, hot peppers), cinnamons, and strong seasonings that trigger facial erythema
Alcohol, particularly red wine, which causes marked vasodilation and prolongs rosacea flares
Very hot foods that vasodilate facial capillaries and promote the inflammatory cycles driving sebaceous hyperplasia

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is beneficial for general health and does not worsen rhinophyma. However, vigorous exercise raises core body temperature, which can trigger rosacea flushing; exercising in cooler environments, choosing cool-water sports, using a fan, and staying well hydrated can reduce this trigger. After CO2 laser surgery, strenuous exercise should be avoided for 2–4 weeks to minimise wound disruption and haematoma formation.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical rosacea treatment (ivermectin, metronidazole, or azelaic acid) consistently every day — irregular use reduces its ability to slow disease progression.
Apply sunscreen every morning, even in cloudy weather, as cloud cover blocks only 20–30% of UV radiation.
Monitor the nasal surface monthly — any new firm nodule, ulceration, or bleeding outside the pattern of typical rhinophyma should be reported to a dermatologist promptly for biopsy.
Take photographs every 6 months under similar lighting conditions to detect subtle progression.
If isotretinoin has been prescribed, use reliable contraception throughout, attend monthly blood tests for lipids and liver function, and avoid donating blood for 30 days after stopping.
prognosis: Without treatment, rhinophyma progresses slowly over years to decades, with the nasal enlargement becoming increasingly pronounced, potentially causing nasal obstruction and significant psychosocial morbidity. With surgical treatment — particularly CO2 laser ablation — cosmetic and functional outcomes are excellent, with satisfaction rates of 85–95% in published series. Recurrence occurs in 5–15% of surgically treated patients within 5 years, and is minimised by ongoing medical management of rosacea and, in some cases, maintenance low-dose isotretinoin. There is a small but real risk of basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma arising within rhinophymatous tissue; published series report an incidence of approximately 3–10% when biopsy is taken from all excised specimens, underscoring the importance of histological examination. Patients who maintain consistent rosacea therapy after surgery have lower recurrence rates than those who discontinue treatment.
recoveryTimeline: After CO2 laser ablation or electrosurgical resurfacing, the nasal surface is raw and weeping for the first 3–5 days; re-epithelialisation is complete within 2–4 weeks. Erythema and mild swelling persist for 4–6 weeks. The full cosmetic result — including fading of redness and settling of contour — is typically visible at 3–6 months post-operatively. Most patients return to normal activities within 1–2 weeks, avoiding vigorous exercise for 3–4 weeks. After scalpel excision with grafting, recovery takes 4–6 weeks. Rosacea maintenance treatment should be resumed 4–6 weeks after the nasal wound has fully healed.
complications: Nasal airway obstruction in advanced disease, causing difficulty breathing through the nose and potentially sleep-disordered breathing requiring surgical correction.
Secondary bacterial infection of the enlarged, sebaceous-plug-filled pores, presenting as crusting, tenderness, and discharge.

Malignant transformation — basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma arise within rhinophymatous tissue in an estimated 3–10% of patients; all surgically excised specimens should undergo histological examination.

Psychosocial complications including social isolation, depression, and stigma — the visible disfigurement carries significant quality-of-life burden that may require psychological support alongside dermatological treatment.
Surgical complications including scarring, skin contour irregularity, and depigmentation, particularly with electrosurgery or cryotherapy if depth control is imprecise.
Recurrence after surgery if underlying rosacea is not suppressed with ongoing medical treatment.

supportResources.name: National Rosacea Society (NRS)
supportResources.description: US-based patient organisation providing comprehensive information on rosacea and rhinophyma, trigger diaries, treatment guides, and peer support.

supportResources.name: Changing Faces (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting people with visible facial differences, including rhinophyma, with psychological resources, peer support, and workplace guidance.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Patient Information Leaflets
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient leaflets on rosacea and phymatous changes, written for a UK audience.

sources.title: Gollnick H et al. Global ROSacea COnsensus (ROSCO) panel: recommendations on subtype classification. Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology, 2018
van Zuuren EJ et al. Interventions for rosacea. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2019
Lim JJ, Bhatt DL. Rhinophyma. StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf, 2023
Krauss A et al. Rhinophyma: clinical characteristics, therapeutic options and outcome. Journal of the German Society of Dermatology, 2022
National Rosacea Society — Rosacea Review and Treatment Guidelines
Tan J et al. Updating the diagnosis, classification and assessment of rosacea: recommendations from the global ROSacea COnsensus (ROSCO) 2017 panel. British Journal of Dermatology, 2017
Cribier B. Pathophysiology of rosacea: red face, why? Annales de Dermatologie et de Vénéréologie, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rhinophyma is a late-stage complication of rosacea, not a separate disease. Rosacea without phymatous change causes facial erythema and papulopustules but does not produce the nasal soft-tissue enlargement characteristic of rhinophyma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Basal cell carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BCC can arise within rhinophymatous tissue or on the nose independently, presenting as a pearly, ulcerated, or bleeding nodule. Any discrete, rapidly growing, or ulcerated lesion on a rhinophymatous nose requires biopsy to exclude malignancy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Squamous cell carcinoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SCC occurs in sun-damaged or chronically inflamed nasal skin and can mimic or complicate rhinophyma; it typically presents as a firm, keratotic, or ulcerated nodule rather than the smooth lobular change of typical rhinophyma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus (lupus pernio)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus pernio — cutaneous sarcoidosis involving the nose — produces violaceous, indurated nasal skin plaques that can enlarge the nose, but differs from rhinophyma by lacking sebaceous hyperplasia and showing non-caseating granulomas on biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: The direct precursor and underlying condition of rhinophyma; all rhinophyma arises in the setting of longstanding rosacea, making rosacea control the cornerstone of prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Acne vulgaris
relatedConditions.relevance: Acne shares sebaceous gland hyperactivity and follicular plugging with rhinophyma and occasionally requires similar systemic retinoid treatment, though its pathophysiology and demographics differ.

relatedConditions.name: Squamous cell carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: SCC and BCC can develop within rhinophymatous nasal tissue and must be excluded by histological examination of all surgically removed specimens.

relatedConditions.name: Basal cell carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The most frequently reported malignancy arising within rhinophyma; accounts for up to 3–10% of excised specimens, underscoring the need for routine histopathology.

faqs.question: What is rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma is a type of rosacea in which the skin of the nose becomes thickened, enlarged, and lumpy due to overgrowth of oil glands and connective tissue. It produces a bulbous or irregular nasal appearance and occurs almost exclusively in men over 50 with long-standing rosacea.

faqs.question: Is rhinophyma caused by drinking alcohol?
faqs.answer: No. Alcohol does not cause rhinophyma. The false association stems from the similar red-face appearance of both conditions. Rhinophyma is caused by rosacea and androgen-driven sebaceous gland overgrowth. However, alcohol is a rosacea trigger that can worsen inflammation and accelerate progression.

faqs.question: Can rhinophyma be cured?
faqs.answer: Established rhinophyma cannot be reversed by medication alone but can be effectively treated with surgical reshaping — CO2 laser ablation or electrosurgical excision. Cosmetic satisfaction rates are 85–95%. Ongoing rosacea treatment after surgery reduces the risk of recurrence, which occurs in about 5–15% of cases.

faqs.question: What does rhinophyma look like?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma makes the nose appear enlarged, irregular, and lumpy — typically red, with visibly enlarged pores and a waxy or greasy surface. The nasal tip and sides are most affected, giving a cauliflower-like contour in severe cases. Visible small blood vessels (telangiectasias) are usually present.

faqs.question: How is rhinophyma treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines medical rosacea control (topical ivermectin, metronidazole, or oral isotretinoin) to slow progression with surgical reshaping for established disease. CO2 laser ablation is the most widely used surgical technique, removing excess tissue in a single session and producing excellent cosmetic results in 85–95% of patients.

faqs.question: Is rhinophyma cancerous?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma itself is benign, but basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma can develop within the thickened nasal tissue in approximately 3–10% of cases. Any ulcerated, bleeding, or rapidly growing area on a rhinophymatous nose should be biopsied to exclude skin cancer.

faqs.question: Why does rhinophyma affect mainly men?
faqs.answer: Male sex hormones — particularly dihydrotestosterone — stimulate sebaceous gland growth and sebum production, driving the sebaceous hyperplasia central to rhinophyma. The male-to-female ratio is approximately 12:1. Women account for fewer than 10% of cases and often have androgen excess when they do develop the condition.

faqs.question: What is the best surgery for rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: CO2 laser ablation is considered the gold standard, offering precise layer-by-layer tissue removal, simultaneous haemostasis, and an 85–95% cosmetic satisfaction rate. Electrosurgical excision produces comparable results in experienced hands. Scalpel excision with grafting is reserved for the most severe cases.

faqs.question: How long does rhinophyma surgery recovery take?
faqs.answer: After CO2 laser ablation, the skin re-epithelialises within 2–4 weeks and redness settles over 4–6 weeks. Most people return to normal daily activities within 1–2 weeks. The full cosmetic result is visible at 3–6 months post-operatively.

faqs.question: Does rhinophyma cause nasal obstruction?
faqs.answer: In advanced cases, yes. The enlarging soft tissue can distort the external nasal valve and narrow the nasal airway, causing mouth breathing and sleep disruption. This is a functional indication for surgery in addition to the cosmetic concern.

faqs.question: Can rhinophyma come back after laser surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in about 5–15% of patients within 5 years. The risk is higher in those who discontinue rosacea treatment after surgery. Ongoing topical therapy and, in some cases, maintenance low-dose isotretinoin can significantly reduce the chance of regrowth.

faqs.question: Can rhinophyma be prevented?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma can often be prevented by diagnosing and consistently treating rosacea early, using daily broad-spectrum sunscreen, avoiding rosacea triggers (sun, alcohol, heat, spicy food), and attending annual dermatology review if you have rosacea and are male. Phymatous changes detected early respond better to isotretinoin and require less extensive surgery.

faqs.question: Is isotretinoin used for rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Yes, at low doses. Isotretinoin shrinks sebaceous glands by up to 75–90%, reducing early rhinophyma in about 50–70% of patients after 4–6 months. It is most useful before or after surgery to reduce recurrence. It requires reliable contraception in women due to teratogenicity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rhinophyma and rosacea?
faqs.answer: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes facial redness, papules, and pustules. Rhinophyma is a late complication of rosacea in which the nasal skin undergoes irreversible sebaceous and connective tissue overgrowth. Rosacea can occur without rhinophyma, but rhinophyma does not occur without rosacea.

faqs.question: Does rhinophyma hurt?
faqs.answer: Rhinophyma is usually painless. Some patients notice tenderness when the enlarged tissue is pressed or when secondary infection occurs. The psychological distress from the visible change is often more significant than any physical discomfort.

faqs.question: Can women get rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Yes, but it is rare — women account for fewer than 10% of rhinophyma cases. The strong male predominance reflects androgen-driven sebaceous hyperplasia. Women who develop rhinophyma should be evaluated for underlying androgen excess or hormonal abnormalities.

faqs.question: How do I find a surgeon for rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Look for a dermatological surgeon, plastic surgeon, or ENT surgeon with specific rhinophyma experience and access to a CO2 laser. Ask how many rhinophyma procedures they perform per year and whether all tissue is sent for histological examination. A dermatologist should co-manage the medical rosacea treatment before and after any surgery.

faqs.question: Does sunscreen help rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: Sunscreen does not reverse rhinophyma but daily SPF 30+ broad-spectrum sunscreen is one of the most effective ways to reduce rosacea flares and slow UV-mediated skin damage that contributes to sebaceous and connective tissue remodelling. It should be applied every morning regardless of weather.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about rhinophyma?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist if you have rosacea and notice gradual nasal thickening, enlarged pores, or a persistent change in nasal contour — early referral allows medical treatment before surgical correction is needed. Seek urgent review if any nasal lesion ulcerates, bleeds persistently, or grows rapidly, as skin cancer must be excluded.

faqs.question: What causes rhinophyma to progress faster?
faqs.answer: Undertreated rosacea, high lifetime sun exposure, heavy alcohol consumption (which triggers rosacea flares), and male sex hormones all accelerate progression. Consistent rosacea treatment, sun protection, and trigger avoidance are the most modifiable factors that slow rhinophyma development.

metaDescription: Rhinophyma is a rosacea complication causing nasal skin thickening and enlargement. Learn about CO2 laser surgery, causes, and how to slow progression.
